I just discovered another actor who played a role on the show Babylon 5 has died. A surprisingly large number of them have died young, to the point there's a meme “Cause of death: acted on Babylon 5”. This seems to relate to the series' heavy emphasis on sacrifice, but this is only a guess.

I'm in the process of rewatching it and am noticing an awful lot of quiet occultism references, which I doubt would be obvious to anyone who hasn't studied occult philosophy and practices. The show also has a lot of meanings, to the extent it's one of the only TV shows I've ever seen which seems to function on the mental; I've also, very bizarrely, caught a glimpse of the spiritual plane while watching one of the episodes.

This seems to me to suggest that something is manifesting through the series, descending from the spiritual through the TV series. This seems to me to play a role in creativity in general, but it seems like it's unusually strong here. The writer and creator of the series is an atheist, so it doesn't seem likely this is intentional, but I wonder: is it possible that by drawing on occultism to a fairly heavy degree he accidentally opened a wider door, so to speak, for whatever is descending from the spiritual through the show?

(Oddly, I first discovered occultism while watching the show for the first time; I wonder now if there's a connection)

For what it's worth, I've felt drawn to rewatch the series recently also, and just discovered that Mira Furlan had died-- for some reason that was particularly saddening. I commented on JMG's post about the Shadow a few months back that Babylon 5 clearly reached beyond the veil. There were other things in addition to the shockingly high rate of casualties among the show's actors-- at one point, the series writer had to agree not to mention actors' body parts in scripts, as they tended to suffer injuries if their characters did.

The writer, JMS-- not to be confused with JMG-- said, regarding an episode where the character Garibaldi, played by Jerry Doyle, broke an arm:


"The funny thing is...in the very next episode after the incident, there was a line in the script I'd written *weeks* earlier, and it freaked everybody out...when Garibaldi asks someone to do something, and the person responds, "What, you've got a broken arm or something?" At first some people thought I'd put it in there to pink Jerry, but it'd been there the whole time. Similarly, in the Claudia incident, there was a line (cut for time) where Sheridan says talking to the Drazi is like trying to talk to your right foot...and Ivanova replies "I'll have you know I have a sublime relationship with my right foot." Yep, the next day...that's the foot she broke.

"Just recently, I was trying to explain time travel to one of the actors. I used the analogy, over lunch, "Suppose you finished eating your chicken here, then got sick as a dog a few hours later, then got in a time machine to go back in time and warn yourself not to eat the chicken." Well, a few hours after that...the actor got sick as a dog from the chicken.

"I have been asked, expressly, not to make any further mention of actors' body parts in scripts...."

comments.title: Spring equinox
comments.content:
A seasonal Q for you JMG!

In “Priestess,” Alan Richardson’s biography of Dion Fortune , he references her writing somewhere that the spring equinox is “always a difficult time for occultists.” Dion further describes an attack that occured during a very difficult astral travel experience during this time.

1. Do you subscribe to the same view? If so could you explain what is behind it?

2. I had a run of bad luck running into the equinox and today while doing 4fold breath prior to my dawn practice of lbrp and MP today I felt an ambiguous presence behind my left shoulder. It felt like all the hairs on my body were standing on end. I rushed to perform lbrp which seemed to calm things.

3. Could this be related to the equinox? Maybe I am just imputing back from what I read or it’s simply a Watcher on threshold thing. Any other folks out there experiencing similar?

4. Either way I assume the best thing to do is just plow on with my regular practice?

Thanks in advance

replies.title: Re: Spring equinox
replies.content:
1) Okay, let's start by summarizing the magical concept of the equinoxes. At each equinox, the Sun and Earth come into a mutual relationship that allows a jolt of energy to descend from the Sun to the Earth. The spring equinox (in either hemisphere) jolts the etheric body of the earth out of the stagnation of winter, so it's always a rough and tumble time. (The autumn equinox is much milder.)

2-3) Might be that, might be something else.

4) Once you get to the point that you can do a solo equinox ritual, that'll help a great deal. Until then, plow on with your regular practice.

comments.content:
Watching the past few years, I've realized that a good way to sum up how life in major cities is evolving is that its heading in the direction of "cyberpunk": decaying infrastructure, ubiquitous access to a severely restricted and dangerous internet mixed with legal and social barriers to in person interactions, governments which are corrupt and extremely totalitarian, and an incredibly artificial environment.

The thing I find fascinating is that cyberpunk burst onto the scene in the late 1970s and early 1980s almost fully formed, at the same time our extended vacation from reality began, and it tends to focus exclusively on urban areas, almost to the extent of ignoring rural areas exist. This always struck me as odd, but now I'm wondering if certain sensitive souls picked up on the future and cyberpunk is a reflection of this. Do you think this is possible?

They are looking to park themselves on any farmland they can, out in the boondocks, willing to work in exchange for a place to stay. Since sensitive people have to keep 7 km away to not feel the effect, they are looking to settle in the relatively narrow strip between the two. I know this is not related to magic, but thought you might be interested.

Dear JMG,

A few weeks ago I asked here about a modified SOP working to buy a new house, which I'm happy to report was successful. I also did some planetary charity to Saturn as well.

Before I started the working, very little was coming on the housing market, and after I started doing it, several houses popped up, and one of them looked uncannily like the house in my visualizations. We put an offer in, but didn't get it, but put another on the next house that fit our criteria and it was accepted. A whole bunch of positive synchronicities came up afterwards along with some further sheer good luck, and nothing major came up in the home inspection.

Part of my intention was to fairly purchase the house - well, the bank sent an appraiser to value the house, and interestingly the house is worth exactly what we are going to pay for it. TS does indeed W!

We have until tomorrow (the 22nd) to rescind our offer, and so to be sure, I cast another Ogham divination to ask 'knowing what we know now, what do we most need to know about moving into and living in the new house?' To my great surprise, I got:

Current situation: Tinne,
Action: Reverse Ngetal,
Outcome: Reverse Straif

So that's not great. I interpreted that to mean that we successfully got the house through decisiveness after competition with other buyers, but now we're going to get stuck in this place, resulting in unhappiness and great difficulty.

I don't know what I overlooked, earlier divination before I did the working and before we offered on the house gave either favorable or mixed results. With such a negative reading, I'm even wondering whether we should rescind our offer. How would you interpret that?

Yet, my intention for the working was 'we fairly purchase a house which best allows us to follow our paths, with positive results for all involved'. Perhaps Reversed Straif is saying 'now you've got the house, it's time to follow the path, and it's going to be really difficult.'

Thank you!

comments.title: Ye Gods
comments.content:
Salutations JMG!

My spirituality is still evolving, but I'm finally ready to start dipping my toes in the waters of traditional polytheist worship. I've selected a one of the Roman gods whose planet is well placed in my chart and whose essential character seems in line with values I cherish, and I'm going to follow the traditional statue-candles-incense-bow-meditate process you've recommended in your Weird of Hali novels and in posts here. But I have some questions. I won't say which god I'm going to approach, so let's just use Mercury as a placeholder.

1) Are Mercury the planet and Mercury the god one and the same, or is there a difference? And if so, what?

2) Would bad chart placements mean I'd have difficulty forming a relationship with Mercury?

3) Where did the traditional neoplatonist devotional practice you put in your novels come from, and where can I read more about it?

4) Considering all the taboos about types of offerings to the Greek gods, where can I read more about the exact offerings and devotional practices for all the Roman gods and goddesses (especially the smaller offerings that individuals were capable of, such as pieces of beef rather than snow white bulls).

5) Are the Roman gods their own distinct entities compared to their Greek predecessors, or are the differences purely Roman civic propaganda pasted over the Greek gods? For example, Venus has almost no myths of her own and absorbed most of her myths from Aphrodite with minimal modification, yet Venus in her Verticordia form presided over virginity and the sanctity of marriage, something which seems very unlike Aphrodite. It's the same or even more puzzling with some of the others I'm most interested in.

6) This question seems a bit silly but...how do I approach the gods? I've always been a bit awkward with meeting new people. Do I start with "Do you like jazz?" or what?

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content:
1) That's a very good question to which I don't know a definite answer. The old Neoplatonists used to teach that the planet is the body of the god.

2) Probably not. Religion is not the same thing as magic.

3) It's not actually that traditional. I made it up out of a combination of scraps of lore from writings on classical Greek religion, on the one hand, and my own experiences on the other.

4) Start with Ovid's Fasti and go from there to scholarly books on Roman religion. One thing to keep in mind is that the Neoplatonists found that all the gods are okay with offerings of frankincense and libations of wine.

5) Even within Greece, Aphrodite had many different forms and powers and manifestations -- Pausanius is a good source for this. Nobody knows why this is.

6) Don't worry about it. The gods already know what you're thinking and feeling. That's why it's a standard practice simply to burn some incense and recite the relevant Orphic hymn; it's a polite and effective way of saying "Hi, I'm interested in a relationship."

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content: 1) Wouldn’t it be then extremely disrespectful to the Moon goddess to put flags on her and claim it as yours in a way? I wonder if any of that had to do with the failed Apollo mission.

replies.content: The US landing crews specifically refrained from claiming the Moon as US territory:  "We came in peace for all mankind" is what the sign on the lander says, and it's surprisingly accurate. I suspect that spared us some blowback.

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content: Oh I’m glad to hear that! I hope future attempts, if any, to carry that same spirit.

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content:

[IMAGE:0]

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content:
1) You've said before that appealing to divinities is a reliable way to overcome bad astrological factors since the gods are above the planets. What I'm really trying to understand with this question is if this implies that the Roman gods are tied to their planets and thus below another set of gods? I'm afraid I don't know enough metaphysics to meditate on this yet.

5) As I understand the spiritual world (in my own idiosyncratic pantheistic way, so take it with a grain of salt), the universe is made by living powers that the gods are individual faces of. Returning to the Aphrodite illustration, the living power of "love giving birth to something new" is like a long mountain range, while Aphrodite, Aine, and Freya are individual mountain peaks. This makes sense of why there are multiple conflicting gods across cultures who all are associated with creating the same part of nature.

What I worry is that, for example, Venus is not a mountain peak of her own, but a Roman rhetorical device to say "All love goddesses are Venus, therefore all your love goddesses are Roman, thus you are Roman and should submit to imperial authority!" Are Jupiter, Saturn, Sol and the rest their own unique beings, or are they a Roman version of the "all gods are one god" tendency that never seems to go so well?

6) For gods without an Orphic hymn, do you still read the Orphic hymn associated with the god's energy (Mercury for Odin for example), do you try to find some other holy text (passages from the Poetic Edda for the Norse, passages from the Mabinogion for the Welsh, etc) or do you skip that part altogether?

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content:
For #5, I don't think this gets fully at the heart of it. The One/The Good is the singular principle that gives rise to all things, and the gods are all distinct, supraessential, fundamentally good individuals clustered in the first rank about The One. They are all outside spacetime and above Being itself, the latter of which includes the physical world; different gods manifest themselves to different locations in the physical world through the symbols of nature. Certain groups of gods will cluster into pantheons (the Greco-Roman one you noted; the Egyptians; in my polytheistic view, the Christian Trinity, etc.)

In short, they are not all different faces of one thing, unless it is The One itself. They are all individuals, some of which will syncretize (play nice) with others, some of which will not.

I heartily recommend Edward Butler's writings on Proclus in this regard.

Again, may the gods forbear any misapprehension or misrepresentation on my part.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content:
1) I'd encourage you to read more metaphysics, and then meditate on the question.

5) That's one of the great questions of polytheist metaphysics, and since we have no independent way of settling facts about the gods, my advice for 1) is also appropriate here.

6) Only use the Orphic hymn for the deity to whom it's addressed.

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content:
One final thought on #5, for boulderchum: IMO, the gods are not representatives of facets of the universe. Facets of the universe testify to the provenance of the gods.

The gods come before the material universe, and indeed it emerges from them, a sort of "consensus reality" among the divinities.

So, for instance, Poseidon is not a "mascot" for the oceans. Instead, the oceans are a symbol (sunthemata) that will lead the properly prepared soul to Poseidon.

Sallustius (a quick read! I prefer the Nock translation, however): https://hermetic.com/texts/on_the_gods-1

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content:
If I may humbly interject on #1...I think there may be some disputation on this. Sallustius, at least, claims that the material objects related to a god are sacred to the gods, but not the gods themselves (wine is sacred to Dionysus, but wine is not Dionysus per se). I conjecture that this would apply to the planets themselves, also--the planet is not the physical god per se, but a sunthemata through which the god becomes manifest. I admit I could be completely off base on this, but speculate that this is why you could have multiple gods (from unrelated pantheons) related to a given symbol.

And may the gods forgive any misapprehension or impiety in my take on this matter.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content: Of course there's disputation. One of the great good things about polytheism is that it treats theology as a subject for entertaining debate, rather than an excuse to slaughter your neighbor. I'm sure most Neoplatonists now would consider the planets to be synthemata, but in ancient times the planets were not thought to be made of ordinary elemental matter, thus were of a higher standing and suited to be divine bodies. At least that's my understanding!

replies.title: Re: Ye Gods
replies.content:
Indeed, this is one of the things I could speculate about all day...and Sallustius does not mention the planets as sunthemata, so you may be right on that one.

Axé!
Fra' Lupo

Hello JMG,

from the point of view of a practicing magician what are the technical differences (in function and outcome) between a banishing ritual and such prayers of protection as the Sub tuum praesidium, the Lorica of Saint Patrick or the prayer to Saint Michael?

Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, let's start from the core occult teaching that there are different planes of existence. Prayers work on the upper end of the astral plane (the plane of concrete thoughts and feelings) and call down influences from the mental and spiritual planes. Banishing rituals do the same thing, but they take those influences further down the planes, through the lower astral to the etheric and physical planes, using movements of the body and figures traced in the air. Thus the rituals tend to be more effective in dealing with forces on the lower astral, etheric, and (in some cases) material planes.

The sacraments of the church do the same thing for their own purposes -- that's why you have to have water for baptism, bread and wine for communion, and so on, rather than just doing a lot of praying.

comments.title: Picactrix and Neoplatonism
comments.content:
Salve, JMG, and thanks as ever for hosting these Luna-day Q&As.

Less a question than an observation: Was rereading Picatrix and struck by its early discussion on the nature of God and how similar it is to Neoplatonism (i.e. the first God being "The One," fundamentally good, its principle feature unity in which everything participates, etc.) This is a pretty remarkable chain of transmission: Iamblichus, Proclus (3rd-4th century) --> Picatrix (10-11th century) --> Ficino, etc. (15th century) and beyond.

I related this back to one reader last week who asked about theurgy, and, while I'm no scholar and this may have been patently obvious to others, it was notable to me given the winding paths of history. Although we don't have 4th century theurgic practices per se, the Neoplatonic framework for how these things operate would seem to remain the same, suggesting that the fundamentals of the operations remain consistent across the centuries.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: Re: Picactrix and Neoplatonism
replies.content: Exactly. Neoplatonism is the theoretical basis for all traditional Western magic, full stop, end of sentence. The Picatrix is a deeply Neoplatonic book, based entirely on the same late classican Neoplatonic synthesis you find in Iamblichus and Proclus -- and yes, that same synthesis can be found just as well in Ficino, Agrippa, and from there onward to the Golden Dawn and beyond.

I meditated on this, and the thought arose that I may not be seeing the bigger picture, because the left-wing intelligentsia had a bigger power behind it that was hidden from view.

I therefore asked Ogham in another single card yes/no divinaton if indeed there was a bigger power behind the left-wing intelligentsia and got Duir - success forward movement - which suggested I was on the right lines. I then asked if this bigger power was a coordinated operation by a foreign government or their agents and got Straif (Rev) - which indicated a "no" as well as pain, difficulty, retribution, a difficult path that must be taken. The "retribution" element of Straif reversed is particularly worth noting, as you will see. I further asked if this power was karmic or spiritual in nature, and got Koad (Rev), which suggested a "no" but that there were possibly too many factors involved to allow successful prediction.

I then asked whether the power involved was a human one and got Huath, which indicated a "yes", and from there whether these humans were foreign and got Nuin, which strongly suggested that I was now in the right ballpark, this nicely contrasting wth the reversed Nuin which indicated that I was isolated and self-contained. I thought for a while about who or what this hidden power could be, and I wondered if the culprit was a globalist organisation such as the World Economic Forum, or one of George Soros' foundations i.e. classic conspiracy territory. I therefore asked whether this power was located in, or based in, the West and got Ailm (Rev) which indicated a "no", and unrealistic ideas yet again.

As this power was obviously non-Western, I then went for the most obvious culprit, and asked if it was in China and got Ailm - expanded awareness, the ability to see things in perspective. This indicated I was bang on target, and contrasted nicely with the original Ailm reversed that had troubled me so. Ailm was a particularly important card in this series of divinations as you will see. This answer was obviously extremely disturbing, as it suggested that phenomena such as critical race theory and "wokeism" may have been deliberately seeded, possibly over decades, by elements in China. I then vaguely recalled that during the Korean War, the Chinese proved particularly adept at brainwashing and "turning" American POW's. I then looked this up on the internet, and the very first link I found was about how this brainwashing was similar to the current strife on the internet. This article is well worth a read on the subtlety of Chinese persuasion techniques:

https://eu.indystar.com/story/opinion/2021/02/02/what-interrogation-american-pows-can-teach-us-social-media-polarization/4334685001/

In fact the US Army were so impressed with Chinese brainwashing techniques that they initiated a plethora of studies on them. Many of these reports are available on the internet, such as this one:

https://scholarworks.lib.csusb.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?referer=https://www.google.com/&httpsredir=1&article=1222&context=etd-project

It should be noted how much the Chinese brainwashing relied on double binds and the systematic evasion of cognitive dissonance - just like wokeism! In concert with this, it should be recalled that during the Cultural Revolution, the Chinese would have learnt how easy it was to overturn a society by radicalising university students with simple but memetic political concepts. If you put these two factors together - the ease with which Americans can be brainwashed, and the ability to overturn society via the universities, you basically have a template for wokeism. It's also worth noting how wokeism even appears very Chinese in its characteristics, with students lambasting professors, struggle sessions and confessions of ideological impurity etc.

Anyway, I then decided to see if I could pinpoint what exactly in China this power was. Further divination yielded the information that it was not ideologically Maoist (Nuin Rev), was not connected to the Security Services (Ruis Rev) or People's Liberation Army (Beith Rev) but that it was connected to (though not necessarily part of) the Chinese State (Duir). This had me flummoxed, but then I wondered that if my suspicion that the Chinese had been acting through the universites was correct then the Education Ministry might be involved. When I asked Ogham about this I got Gort, which suggested that it was in a round about way. I then asked if the power behind the left-wing intelligentsia was connected to a scholarship program, and got Ailm. Bullseye. Wikipedia has a list of scholarships connected to the Education Ministry, and this particular one stuck out like a sore thumb:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schwarzman_Scholars

Notice all the Western luminaries associated with them! If you read the third paragraph of this Wiki article, you will see that this scholarship "maintains ties" to this particularly unlovely organisation:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Front_Work_Department

I then asked Ogham whether this was the ultimate power behind the left-wing intellegentsia and their contempt for their nation and people, and I got:

AILM

So if Ogham, or at least my interpretation of it, is correct, then this is the organisation that is ultimately behind the domestic political ferment in the USA and the West generally. Note that this does not contradict the previous Straif reversed reading that indicated that a foreign government was not involved in this issue, as the above agency is not connected to the state, but answers directly to the inner circle of the CCP. Again, if this is correct, then it fits in with a general pattern of the Chinese quietly and gradually corrupting the USA from within, for example by co-opting its political class:

https://www.tabletmag.com/sections/news/articles/the-thirty-tyrants

Or by subtly interfering with its military procurement:

https://www.theamericanconservative.com/articles/americas-monopoly-crisis-hits-the-military/

This suggests that all the time that China has been pretending to be a passive trade partner to America, it has in fact been waging an all-out, but extremely subtle and gradualist, war against it.

Now all that being said, I cannot be sure that I haven't been leading myself up the garden path with all this, as China is pretty much the least "woke" country in the world, and I am not sure what they have to gain by implanting the opposite ideology in their main rival. One possibility is that the Chinese started this decades ago to exploit America's racial divisions, and at that time they did not countenance the accelaration that would be made possible by the internet and social media. This would explain why they were so quick to isolate their own internet from the rest of the world, and why they are now desperately trying to homogenise their own ethnic minorities such as the Uighurs and Tibetans, in order to avoid blowback from what is happening in the US. At the extreme edge of speculation, this might also explain tbe sudden emergence of Covid.

Nonetheless I would treat all the above with a due measure of scepticism, but it does indicate at least a possibility of what might have been going on under all our noses.

replies.content:
I am impressed by the OP's results, and suspect that he is quite right about the UFWD's deliberate activities.

The Western history that Tolkienguy mentions did indeed happen much as he described it. I had a colleague at Brown, a very conservaticve Catholic, who once had to share an office with Herbert Marcuse for an number of years, and boy! did he give me an earful about that guy. Other colleagues, in other departments at Brown, were very much influenced by Marcuse, Gramsci, the Frankfurt School and even--in a different direction--by the Black Panthers. But there was no internal consistency--no traces of any organizing hand at work behind the scenes--in how this influence played out in their academic lives and their teaching. So all the developments Tolkienguy mentions are part of the picture, but they are not its center.

What is going on nowadays seems much more closely and tightly organized to me, and I, too, have been wondering whether it is the result of a sophisticated operation by some outside agency with a vast budget and almost umlimited power. So China's UFWD fits my suspicions, though like the OP I had never heard of it before. (The Wikipedia article that he cites is verrrry interesting.)

One more thing. One of the real blind spots in Western global-strategic thinking seems to me to be the thoughtless assumption that all the most significant players in the great game are either actual nation-states (the USA, Russia, China, Japan, Brazil, etc.) or would-be nation-states (the Islamic State, for example). No, they aren't, not any longer. That assumption falls under the head of "fighting the previous generation's war."

The players that really matter more than all but a few governments these days are of two sorts: very large corporations in the fields of media and energy (which often have more wealth than most nation-states), and looser congeries of various outlaw economies (smuggling networks, drug cartels, etc. etc.).

There is quite a bit of extremely sophisticated research going on now about how people ("consumers," often as not, but not only them) can be powerfully influenced in their actions without their conscious awareness of why they are doing what they are doing. Some of these researchers are publishing their work in academic venues (some of them are even professors at the Harvard Business School, which has a couple of researchers whose work I am keeping a desultory eye on); others make it available only in-house to select employees. Some of these researchers have even been working out the ways that rituals are a very powerful tool for this purpose, and have been trying to apply their results in business.

It's only a matter of time before researchers in the West figure out how to weaponize several things of this sort, such as ritual activity and the placebo/nocebo response. Combine the two and you might even be able to come up with a sort of super-weapon that could use the nocebo effect to kill heads opf state and similar powerful people in ways that seem "magical" to ordinary people (in effect, targeted strategic assassinations). We're not there yet, to judge by the published work I have seen, but it seems to me that we're quite likely to get there in another decade or two.

And that's the situation here in the relatively open Western world. In China and in the Soviet Union intense research along these lines has apparently been going on for at least half a century now. There it's all highly classified, of course, but Brown University was a sort of academic "Spook Central" for decades in the years during and after WW2, and a fair number of my academic colleagues in the early days were former operatives in the OSS and its successor. the CIA--or in some cases were simply very close friends (close as in "fishing buddies") with highly placed men in the US intelligence community. Occasionally these colleagues would be so indiscreet as to drop a tiny hint or two in response to my life-long curiosity about such things (which is strongly connected to my life-long curiosity about magic and occultism).

Chigi Chapel, in which the Creator God is surrounded by the Seven and their attending angels: https://www.123rf.com/photo_77718369_the-cupola-in-chigi-chapel-designed-by-raphael-painting-of-the-creation-story-by-francesco-salviati-.html

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There are ways for atheism to reconcile that kind of experiences. You just have to consider that there are higher levels of complexity above yourself, stop being so 'humanistic'. Your body is one very high complex system, your group of friends is rather simple system but it's made of complex systems, the whole humanity is another, and so with any complex system. Each of these systems have their own emergent properties, that is, properties that are only meaningful when taken at that level of complexity. Just consider the possibility that some of these properties are consciousness and self-awareness, and you'll have a working model for what theists call 'gods'.

Well, a proper reductionist skeptic would not accept their existence even when he gets a formal written declaration from the being itself. If I were invisible and some bossy person asked me a proof of my existence, I could give him a punch, but when asked a second time, which the skeptic must do since he is conducting a scientific experiment, I'd simply ignore the request. And the skeptic will indoubtably conclude that out of 100 tests, only one showed some weird value, and so it must be considered an error. Moral of the story, reductionism only works with non conscious systems.

A not so skeptic atheist will consider such manifestations the same way as the color of things or the temperatures. These are properties that arise when the conditions are right. In the case of color, you need sufficient matter of a given size for the electromagnetic rays to be deflected or absorbed in some ways, to cause a disturbance in the electromagnetic radiation our eyes are able to detect. Consciousness requires a high level of complexity, at least as complex as a human body, but not restricted to it. Find systems with such complexities, and they are likely to have some sort of consciousness of their own, and maybe some other emergent properties we aren't able to understand (not just perceive!), given our level.
(Note, complexity is not just about sizes, but about number of relationships).

I'd say that the major difference is that a theist says that god(s) exist, where the non skeptic atheist says that these are things that naturally emerge. Keep increasing the complexity in a machine and eventually it will gain consciousness.

comments.title: Energy currents
comments.content:
I went to the beach for a week with my family and one of the things I did, trying to get in contact with Water, was to spend a lot of time among the waves. Needless to say an immense sense of respect for the ocean came to me not only because of its vastness but because of its might. There is nothing like feeling the whole thing around and beneath you shifting and rising in tides and waves.

Of course the next thing that I needed to do was learn how to surf, and so I did. I noticed something interesting though as good Mario the lifeguard was teaching me how to stand on a surfboard. As I was fumbling with the board while what seemed to me huge waves breaking behind me and Mario going through what I needed to do I felt as if I was being initiated into something. I dismissed it as a vagary but when I first stood firmly on top of the board I distinctively felt my consciousness shifted into something different.

After a long afternoon of catching waves (what a feeling!) I started to think about what initiation is. Once you described initiatory orders, I think, as currents of energy. I thought of this as merely magical in nature but this experience makes me think that initiation is something natural to human beings and we get initiated into several currents be getting into contact with someone that belongs to that current and going through some simple steps. Of course, I would assume, occultists paid attention to this and perfected it into a powerful means to get in contact with magical currents but isn’t it then initiation the human way of passing on what hundreds before us have done and experienced? Perhaps, I was initiated into The Order of the Barreling Wave? The jumping dolphins I watched afterwards through my binoculars made me feel as such and that as a welcome.

When I got back home I also noticed something different. My house, after a week inhabited, it had the distinctive feel of deadness that empty houses have. What is this? Perhaps houses get charged and enlivened by their inhabitants? I did the LSRP in my room and it sparked to life again!

But then there is surely one moment when you have to push through. If you don't, you'll bounce below the ceiling. But if you do, something happens, something changes. JMG,

I've been pondering the nature of gods for a while now. Check me on this. As near as we mere mortals can tell.

1) Gods are born, grow in strength, wane in strength and then die.
2) This process may be thousands of years in length.
3) We call gods by various names, but our choice of names is not necessarily related to one god. For example, there are likely many gods answering the name of Jesus.
4) Gods can be connected to geographic locations, a particular people, or a culture.
5) But they can also move, transfer to other cultures, or peoples.
6) It is likely that the Odin worshipped today is not the same Odin of the Germanic people BC.
7) But the Odin of today may have many similar traits to the ancient Odin.

Am I off base on any of these?

AV

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Those all seem to be more or less true. As I see it, what's behind those facts is something like this:

1) Gods are eternal spiritual powers. "Eternal" means outside of time.
2) We are within time, and so what we perceive of gods is their reflections in time, not the gods as they are in themselves.
3) The reflections of the gods have all the complexities you've described, since things in time necessarily are born, grow, age, die, move around, et al.
4) Each god can cast many different reflections in different places, times, and cultures.
5) The god is the same but the reflections are different, and the reflections are real beings within time, just as we are.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's more complex than that, because the pentagram and hexagram rituals aren't complete in and of themselves. To use GD magic for practical purposes, you perform an opening ritual which begins with the LBRP and includes several other steps, then you perform a pentagram or hexagram ritual to awaken some specific magical energy, then you do something with the energy -- consecrate a talisman, summon and communicate with a spirit, or what have you. Then you banish the leftover energy you awakened, and perform a closing ritual which ends with the LBRP.
